During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace collected system information. Group 'Play': Play has leveraged tools to enumerate system information. Group 'Storm-0501': Storm-0501 has leveraged native Windows tools and commands such as `systeminfo` and open-source tools including OSQuery and ossec-win32 to query details about the endpoint. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has configured malicious webpages to identify the victim’s operating system by reviewing the details of the victims User-Agent of their browser. Group 'APT42': APT42 has used malware, such as GHAMBAR and POWERPOST, to collect system information. Group 'SideCopy': SideCopy has identified the OS version of a compromised host. Group 'APT19': APT19 collected system architecture information. APT19 used an HTTP malware variant and a Port 22 malware variant to gather the hostname and CPU information from the victim’s machine. Group 'APT18': APT18 can collect system information from the victim’s machine. Group 'FIN8': FIN8 has used PowerShell Scripts to check the architecture of a compromised machine before the selection of a 32-bit or 64-bit version of a malicious .NET loader. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has detected a target system’s OS version. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has used native OS commands to understand privilege levels and system details. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern script execution includes basic victim information gathering steps which are then transmitted to command and control servers. Group 'Mustard Tempest': Mustard Tempest has used implants to perform system reconnaissance on targeted systems. Group 'Sowbug': Sowbug obtained OS version and hardware configuration from a victim. Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has used Systeminfo and similar commands to acquire detailed configuration information of a victim's machine. Wizard Spider has also utilized the PowerShell cmdlet `Get-ADComputer` to collect DNS hostnames, last logon dates, and operating system information from Active Directory. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover operating system configuration details using the systeminfo and set commands. Group 'Patchwork': Patchwork collected the victim computer name, OS version, and architecture type and sent the information to its C2 server. Group 'Windshift': Windshift has used malware to identify the computer name of a compromised host. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following commands after exploiting a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about the OS: ver >> %temp%\download systeminfo >> %temp%\download Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has leveraged `cmd.exe` to identify system info `cmd.exe /c systeminfo`. Group 'Stealth Falcon': Stealth Falcon malware gathers system information via WMI, including the system directory, build number, serial number, version, manufacturer, model, and total physical memory. Group 'Moonstone Sleet': Moonstone Sleet has gathered information on victim systems. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team used a backdoor to enumerate information about the infected system's operating system. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang performs operating system information discovery using systeminfo and has used implants to identify the system language and computer name. Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan Australian Intrusions was conducted by the Leviathan threat actor.Leviathan performed host enumeration and data gathering operations on victim machines during Leviathan Australian Intrusions. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity includes use of native system tools, such as uname, to obtain information about victim device architecture, as well as gathering other system information such as the victim's hosts file and CPU utilization. Campaign 'Operation AkaiRyū': During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace collected system information. Campaign 'Frankenstein': During Frankenstein, the threat actors used Empire to obtain the compromised machine's name. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `systeminfo` command to gather details about a compromised system. Campaign 'Leviathan Australian Intrusions': Leviathan performed host enumeration and data gathering operations on victim machines during Leviathan Australian Intrusions. Campaign 'RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations': Mustang Panda captured victim operating system type via User Agent analysis during RedDelta Modified PlugX Infection Chain Operations. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, the threat actors collected the computer name, OS, and other system information using `cmd /c systeminfo > %temp%\ temp.ini`. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors fingerprinted targeted SharePoint servers to identify OS version and running processes. Campaign 'Juicy Mix': During Juicy Mix, OilRig used a script to send the name of the compromised host via HTTP `POST` to register it with C2. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors discovered the OS versions of systems connected to a targeted network. Campaign 'Cutting Edge': During Cutting Edge, threat actors used the ENUM4LINUX Perl script for discovery on Windows and Samba hosts. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary tasked Claude Code to query databases and systems in order to identify proprietary information, including system configurations and database types. Campaign 'ArcaneDoor': ArcaneDoor included collection of victim device configuration information. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity includes use of native system tools, such as uname, to obtain information about victim device architecture, as well as gathering other system information such as the victim's hosts file and CPU utilization. Campaign 'FunnyDream': During FunnyDream, the threat actors used Systeminfo to collect information on targeted hosts.

T1053.002.name: At
T1053.002.description: Adversaries may abuse the [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) utility to perform task scheduling for initial or recurring execution of malicious code. The [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) utility exists as an executable within Windows, Linux, and macOS for scheduling tasks at a specified time and date. Although deprecated in favor of [Scheduled Task](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1053/005)'s [schtasks](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0111) in Windows environments, using [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) requires that the Task Scheduler service be running, and the user to be logged on as a member of the local Administrators group. In addition to explicitly running the `at` command, adversaries may also schedule a task with [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) by directly leveraging the [Windows Management Instrumentation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1047) `Win32_ScheduledJob` WMI class.(Citation: Malicious Life by Cybereason) On Linux and macOS, [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) may be invoked by the superuser as well as any users added to the at.allow file. If the at.allow file does not exist, the at.deny file is checked. Every username not listed in at.deny is allowed to invoke [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110). If the at.deny exists and is empty, global use of [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) is permitted. If neither file exists (which is often the baseline) only the superuser is allowed to use [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110).(Citation: Linux at) Adversaries may use [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) to execute programs at system startup or on a scheduled basis for [Persistence](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0003). [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) can also be abused to conduct remote [Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0002) as part of [Lateral Movement](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0008) and/or to run a process under the context of a specified account (such as SYSTEM). In Linux environments, adversaries may also abuse [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) to break out of restricted environments by using a task to spawn an interactive system shell or to run system commands. Similarly, [at](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0110) may also be used for [Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0004) if the binary is allowed to run as superuser via sudo.(Citation: GTFObins at)
T1053.002.mitigations: Mitigation 'Operating System Configuration': Configure settings for scheduled tasks to force tasks to run under the context of the authenticated account instead of allowing them to run as SYSTEM. The associated Registry key is located at HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\SubmitControl. The setting can be configured through GPO: Computer Configuration > [Policies] > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options: Domain Controller: Allow server operators to schedule tasks, set to disabled. Mitigation 'Audit': Toolkits like the PowerSploit framework contain PowerUp modules that can be used to explore systems for permission weaknesses in scheduled tasks that could be used to escalate privileges. Windows operating system also creates a registry key specifically associated with the creation of a scheduled task on the destination host at: Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\At1. In Linux and macOS environments, scheduled tasks using at can be audited locally, or through centrally collected logging, using syslog, or auditd events from the host. Mitigation 'User Account Management': Limit privileges of user accounts and remediate Privilege Escalation vectors so only authorized administrators can create scheduled tasks on remote systems. In Linux environments, users account-level access to at can be managed using at.allow and at.deny files. Users listed in the at.allow are enabled to schedule actions using at, whereas users listed in at.deny file disabled from the utility. Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': Configure the Increase Scheduling Priority option to only allow the Administrators group the rights to schedule a priority process. This can be configured through GPO: Computer Configuration > [Policies] > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment: Increase scheduling priority.
T1053.002.detections:
Detection Strategy: Cross-Platform Detection of Scheduled Task/Job Abuse via `at` Utility
- Analytic 'Analytic 0943': Detects creation of scheduled tasks via `at.exe` or WMI `Win32_ScheduledJob` class, followed by execution of anomalous processes by svchost.exe or taskeng.exe.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0944': Detects usage of `at` command to schedule jobs, followed by job execution and modification of job files under /var/spool/cron/atjobs.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0945': Detects user or root invocation of `at` command to schedule a job, followed by job execution using LaunchServices and activity in /usr/lib/cron/at.
T1053.002.procedures:
Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can set a scheduled task on the target system to execute commands remotely using at.
Software 'at': at can be used to schedule a task on a system to be executed at a specific date or time.
Software 'MURKYTOP': MURKYTOP has the capability to schedule remote AT jobs.
Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 actors use at to schedule tasks to run self-extracting RAR archives, which install HTTPBrowser or PlugX on other victims on a network.
Group 'APT18': APT18 actors used the native at Windows task scheduler tool to use scheduled tasks for execution on a victim network.
Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used at to register a scheduled task to execute malware during lateral movement.

T1083.name: File and Directory Discovery
T1083.description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories or may search in specific locations of a host or network share for certain information within a file system. Adversaries may use the information from [File and Directory Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1083) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. Many command shell utilities can be used to obtain this information. Examples include dir, tree, ls, find, and locate.(Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) Custom tools may also be used to gather file and directory information and interact with the [Native API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1106). Adversaries may also leverage a [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) on network devices to gather file and directory information (e.g. dir, show flash, and/or nvram).(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A) Some files and directories may require elevated or specific user permissions to access.
T1083.detections:
Detection Strategy: Recursive Enumeration of Files and Directories Across Privilege Contexts
- Analytic 'Analytic 1040': Execution of file enumeration commands (e.g., 'dir', 'tree') from non-standard processes or unusual user contexts, followed by recursive directory traversal or access to sensitive locations.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1041': Use of file enumeration commands (e.g., 'ls', 'find', 'locate') executed by suspicious users or scripts accessing broad file hierarchies or restricted directories.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1042': Execution of file or directory discovery commands (e.g., 'ls', 'find') from terminal or script-based tooling, especially outside normal user workflows.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1043': Execution of esxcli commands to enumerate datastore, configuration files, or directory structures by unauthorized or remote users.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1044': Execution of file discovery commands (e.g., 'dir', 'show flash', 'nvram:') from CLI interfaces, especially by unauthorized users or from abnormal source IPs.
T1083.procedures: Software 'Sliver': Sliver can enumerate files on a target system. Software 'cmd': cmd can be used to find files and directories with native functionality such as dir commands. Software 'Rclone': Rclone can list files and directories with the `ls`, `lsd`, and `lsl` commands. Software 'Empire': Empire includes various modules for finding files of interest on hosts and network shares. Software 'Pupy': Pupy can walk through directories and recursively search for strings in files. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate files on the local file system and includes a module for enumerating recently accessed files. Software 'Koadic': Koadic can obtain a list of directories. Software 'Forfiles': Forfiles can be used to locate certain types of files/directories in a system.(ex: locate all files with a specific extension, name, and/or age) Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can discover specified filetypes and log files on a targeted system. Software 'TruffleHog': TruffleHog has can browse and scan individual files and directories. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY has several modules, such as `ls.py`, `pwd.py`, and `recentFiles.py`, to enumerate directories and files. Software 'RemoteUtilities': RemoteUtilities can enumerate files and directories on a target machine. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can search for files on the infected machine. Software 'Imminent Monitor': Imminent Monitor has a dynamic debugging feature to check whether it is located in the %TEMP% directory, otherwise it copies itself there. Software 'Diskpart': If executed with elevated privileges, Diskpart can list all volumes, including virtual disks. Software 'BLACKCOFFEE': BLACKCOFFEE has the capability to enumerate files. Software 'Orz': Orz can gather victim drive information. Software 'Attor': Attor has a plugin that enumerates files with specific extensions on all hard disk drives and stores file information in encrypted log files. Software 'USBStealer': USBStealer searches victim drives for files matching certain extensions (“.skr”,“.pkr” or “.key”) or names. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to get directory listings or drive information on a compromised host. Software 'Kinsing': Kinsing has used the find command to search for specific files. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has searched for folders associated with antivirus software. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can list all files and their associated attributes, including filename, type, owner, creation time, last access time, last write time, size, and permissions. Software 'PlugX': PlugX has a module to enumerate drives and find files recursively. PlugX has also checked the path from which it is running for specific parameters prior to execution. Software 'Akira': Akira examines files prior to encryption to determine if they meet requirements for encryption and can be encrypted by the ransomware. These checks are performed through native Windows functions such as GetFileAttributesW. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer can list directories on a victim. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag AshenOrchestrator component can enumerate files on victim hosts. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell has the ability to list files and file characteristics including extension, size, ownership, and permissions. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can enumerate the victim's desktop. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains commands to list files and directories, as well as search for files matching certain extensions from a defined list. Software 'SynAck': SynAck checks its directory location in an attempt to avoid launching in a sandbox. Software 'yty': yty gathers information on victim’s drives and has a plugin for document listing. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': TAINTEDSCRIBE can use DirectoryList to enumerate files in a specified directory. Software 'ELMER': ELMER is capable of performing directory listings. Software 'SUGARDUMP': SUGARDUMP can search for and collect data from specific Chrome, Opera, Microsoft Edge, and Firefox files, including any folders that have the string `Profile` in its name. Software 'Fysbis': Fysbis has the ability to search for files. Software 'Remsec': Remsec is capable of listing contents of folders on the victim. Remsec also searches for custom network encryption software on victims. Software 'KillDisk': KillDisk has used the FindNextFile command as part of its file deletion process. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has identified specific directories and files for exfiltration using the `ssh_upload` command which contains subcommands of `.sdira`, `sdir`, `sfile`, `sfinda`, `sfindr`, `sfind`. InvisibleFerret also has the capability to scan and upload files of interest from multiple OS systems through the use of scripts that check file names, file extensions, and avoids certain path names. InvisibleFerret has utilized the `findstr` on Windows or the macOS `find` commands to search for files of interest. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has searched for folders, subfolders and other networked or mounted drives for follow on encryption actions. Embargo has also iterated device volumes using `FindFirstVolumeW()` and `FindNextVolumeW()` functions and then calls the `GetVolumePathNamesForVolumeNameW()` function to retrieve a list of drive letters and mounted folder paths for each specified volume. Software 'WinMM': WinMM sets a WH_CBT Windows hook to search for and capture files on the victim. Software 'Turian': Turian can search for specific files and list directories. Software 'POORAIM': POORAIM can conduct file browsing. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia can perform a recursive directory listing for all volume drives available on the victim's machine and can also fetch specific files by their paths. Software 'Misdat': Misdat is capable of running commands to obtain a list of files and directories, as well as enumerating logical drives. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows can check for the presence of specific files prior to moving to the next phase of execution. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information from the victim, including installed drivers, programs previously executed by users, programs and services configured to automatically run at startup, files and folders present in any user's home folder, files and folders present in any user's My Documents, programs installed to the Program Files folder, and recently accessed files, folders, and programs. Software 'BoxCaon': BoxCaon has searched for files on the system, such as documents located in the desktop folder. Software 'LazyWiper': LazyWiper can specifically target multiple files by extension including: .rar, .tar.gz, .zip, .7z, .json, .bcp, .bak, .gho, .erf, .edb, .onepkg, .pst, and .ldiff. Software 'njRAT': njRAT can browse file systems using a file manager module. Software 'PinchDuke': PinchDuke searches for files created within a certain timeframe and whose file extension matches a predefined list. Software 'WindTail': WindTail has the ability to enumerate the users home directory and the path to its own application bundle. Software 'AuditCred': AuditCred can search through folders and files on the system. Software 'Diavol': Diavol has a command to traverse the files and directories in a given path. Software 'PowerDuke': PowerDuke has commands to get the current directory name as well as the size of a file. It also has commands to obtain information about logical drives, drive type, and free space. Software 'BackConfig': BackConfig has the ability to identify folders and files related to previous infections. Software 'Bankshot': Bankshot searches for files on the victim's machine. Software 'PingPull': PingPull can enumerate storage volumes and folder contents of a compromised host. Software '4H RAT': 4H RAT has the capability to obtain file and directory listings. Software 'FALLCHILL': FALLCHILL can search files on a victim. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can look for files carrying specific extensions such as: .rtf, .doc, .docx, .xls, .xlsx, .ppt, .pptx, .pps, .ppsx, .txt, .gpg, .pkr, .kdbx, .key, and .jpb. Software 'Epic': Epic recursively searches for all .doc files on the system and collects a directory listing of the Desktop, %TEMP%, and %WINDOWS%\Temp directories. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can browse directories on a compromised host. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to list the directories on a compromised host. Software 'Ryuk': Ryuk has enumerated files and folders on all mounted drives. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify whether it has been run previously on a host by checking for a specified folder. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT has the ability to list files upon receiving the ls command from C2. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can locate files based on hardcoded file extensions. Software 'Seasalt': Seasalt has the capability to identify the drive type on a victim. Software 'KEYMARBLE': KEYMARBLE has a command to search for files on the victim’s machine. Software 'SHOTPUT': SHOTPUT has a command to obtain a directory listing. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate files and directories. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT checks its current working directory upon execution and also contains watchdog functionality that ensures its executable is located in the correct path (else it will rewrite the payload). Software 'OwaAuth': OwaAuth has a command to list its directory and logical drives. Software 'RARSTONE': RARSTONE obtains installer properties from Uninstall Registry Key entries to obtain information about installed applications and how to uninstall certain applications. Software 'ANELLDR': ANELLDR can enumerate files in the current directory to search for encrypted payload files. Software 'Kivars': Kivars has the ability to list drives on the infected host. Software 'BlackEnergy': BlackEnergy gathers a list of installed apps from the uninstall program Registry. It also gathers registered mail, browser, and instant messaging clients from the Registry. BlackEnergy has searched for given file types. Software 'NightClub': NightClub can use a file monitor to identify .lnk, .doc, .docx, .xls, .xslx, and .pdf files. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb has the ability to retrieve directory listings. Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry searches for variety of user files by file extension before encrypting them using RSA and AES, including Office, PDF, image, audio, video, source code, archive/compression format, and key and certificate files. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang has the ability to list directories. Software 'AutoIt backdoor': AutoIt backdoor is capable of identifying documents on the victim with the following extensions: .doc; .pdf, .csv, .ppt, .docx, .pst, .xls, .xlsx, .pptx, and .jpeg. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell can search for files in directories. Software 'USBferry': USBferry can detect the victim's file or folder list. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon has searched for specific files prior to encryption. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist has the ability to search for specific files. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent has the ability to enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has checked for the existence of specific files including /usr/sbin/setenforce and /etc/selinux/config. It also has the ability to monitor the cryptocurrency miner file and process. Software 'zwShell': zwShell can browse the file system. Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell has a command to open a file manager and explorer on the system. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP has the ability to enumerate directories for files that match a set list. Software 'DropBook': DropBook can collect the names of all files and folders in the Program Files directories. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has the ability to discover drive information on the infected host. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of files and directories in C:\ with the command dir /s /a c:\ >> "C:\windows\TEMP\[RANDOM].tmp". Software 'Ninja': Ninja has the ability to enumerate directory content. Software 'JPIN': JPIN can enumerate drives and their types. It can also change file permissions using cacls.exe. Software 'ZLib': ZLib has the ability to enumerate files and drives. Software 'FLASHFLOOD': FLASHFLOOD searches for interesting files (either a default or customized set of file extensions) on the local system and removable media. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot can search for specific files on an infected host. Software 'HOPLIGHT': HOPLIGHT has been observed enumerating system drives and partitions. Software 'Mango': Mango can enumerate the contents of current working or other specified directories. Software 'BadPatch': BadPatch searches for files with specific file extensions. Software 'NETEAGLE': NETEAGLE allows adversaries to enumerate and modify the infected host's file system. It supports searching for directories, creating directories, listing directory contents, reading and writing to files, retrieving file attributes, and retrieving volume information. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can search files on a compromised host. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can collect desktop filenames. Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can exclude files associated with core system functions from encryption. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has used dir to search for "programfiles" and "appdata". Software 'MoonWind': MoonWind has a command to return a directory listing for a specified directory. Software 'MegaCortex': MegaCortex can parse the available drives and directories to determine which files to encrypt. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer can search for files associated with specific applications. Software 'Pteranodon': Pteranodon identifies files matching certain file extension and copies them to subdirectories it created. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has looked for files in the user's home directory with "wallet" in their name using find. Software 'DynoWiper': DynoWiper has used the Microsoft Windows native `FindFirstFile()` and `FindNextFile()` to recursively enumerate directories and files on the system. Software 'CrossRAT': CrossRAT can list all files on a system. Software 'DEATHRANSOM': DEATHRANSOM can use loop operations to enumerate directories on a compromised host. Software 'InnaputRAT': InnaputRAT enumerates directories and obtains file attributes on a system. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST had commands to enumerate files and directories. Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE can list information of all files in the system recursively from the root directory or from a specified directory. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can enumerate files by using a variety of functions. Software 'Linfo': Linfo creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can list contents of drives and search for files. Software 'CosmicDuke': CosmicDuke searches attached and mounted drives for file extensions and keywords that match a predefined list. Software 'Spica': Spica can list filesystem contents on targeted systems. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar finds a specified directory, lists the files and metadata about those files. Software 'XAgentOSX': XAgentOSX contains the readFiles function to return a detailed listing (sometimes recursive) of a specified directory. XAgentOSX contains the showBackupIosFolder function to check for IOS device backups by running ls -la ~/Library/Application\ Support/MobileSync/Backup/. Software 'HermeticWiper': HermeticWiper can enumerate common folders such as My Documents, Desktop, and AppData. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can use the Linux API `statvfs` to enumerate the current working directory. Software 'FIVEHANDS': FIVEHANDS has the ability to enumerate files on a compromised host in order to encrypt files with specific extensions. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can enumerate specific files for encryption. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can explore files on a compromised system. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman has the ability to enumerate file and folder names. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer’s data wiper component enumerates specific files on all the Windows drives. Software 'Brave Prince': Brave Prince gathers file and directory information from the victim’s machine. Software 'Havoc': The Havoc interface can display a file explorer view of the compromised host. Software 'OceanSalt': OceanSalt can extract drive information from the endpoint and search files on the system. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE has the ability to search for files on the compromised host. Software 'Manjusaka': Manjusaka can gather information about specific files on the victim system. Software 'TINYTYPHON': TINYTYPHON searches through the drive containing the OS, then all drive letters C through to Z, for documents matching certain extensions. Software 'Octopus': Octopus can collect information on the Windows directory and searches for compressed RAR files on the host. Software 'SUNSPOT': SUNSPOT enumerated the Orion software Visual Studio solution directory path. Software 'Zox': Zox can enumerate files on a compromised host. Software 'Elise': A variant of Elise executes dir C:\progra~1 when initially run. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal can retrieve a file listing from the system. Software 'MiniDuke': MiniDuke can enumerate local drives. Software 'Conti': Conti can discover files on a local system. Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA can enumerate files and directories. Software 'ADVSTORESHELL': ADVSTORESHELL can list files and directories. Software 'FinFisher': FinFisher enumerates directories and scans for certain files. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte enumerates all document files on an infected machine, then creates a summary of these items including filename and directory location prior to exfiltration to cloud hosting services. Software 'Pcexter': Pcexter has the ability to search for files in specified directories. Software 'LoFiSe': LoFiSe can monitor the file system to identify files less than 6.4 MB in size with file extensions including .doc, .docx, .xls, .xlsx, .ppt, .pptx, .pdf, .rtf, .tif, .odt, .ods, .odp, .eml, and .msg. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to browse files in directories such as Program Files and the Desktop. Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER will survey the contents of system files during installation. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can enumerate drives and list the contents of the C: drive on a victim's computer. Software 'REvil': REvil has the ability to identify specific files and directories that are not to be encrypted. Software 'LightSpy': LightSpy uses the `NSFileManager` to move, create and delete files. LightSpy can also use the assembly `bt` instruction to determine a file's executable permissions. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can retrieve data from specific Windows directories, as well as open random files as part of Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion. Software 'Proxysvc': Proxysvc lists files in directories. Software 'LockBit 2.0': LockBit 2.0 can exclude files associated with core system functions from encryption. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT collects the volumes mapped on the system, and also steals files with the following extensions: .docx, .doc, .pptx, .ppt, .xlsx, .xls, .rtf, and .pdf. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has a command to list files on a system. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software '3PARA RAT': 3PARA RAT has a command to retrieve metadata for files on disk as well as a command to list the current working directory. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can enumerate files and directories. Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya searches for files ending with dozens of different file extensions prior to encryption. Software 'ThreatNeedle': ThreatNeedle can obtain file and directory information. Software 'BoomBox': BoomBox can search for specific files and directories on a machine. Software 'Samurai': Samurai can use a specific module for file enumeration. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can browse file systems. Software 'Clop': Clop has searched folders and subfolders for files to encrypt. Software 'GoldenSpy': GoldenSpy has included a program "ExeProtector", which monitors for the existence of GoldenSpy on the infected system and redownloads if necessary. Software 'OutSteel': OutSteel can search for specific file extensions, including zipped files. Software 'FYAnti': FYAnti can search the C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\ directory for files of a specified size. Software 'BADFLICK': BADFLICK has searched for files on the infected host. Software 'CaddyWiper': CaddyWiper can enumerate all files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet uses a driver to scan for specific filesystem driver objects. Software 'CHOPSTICK': An older version of CHOPSTICK has a module that monitors all mounted volumes for files with the extensions .doc, .docx, .pgp, .gpg, .m2f, or .m2o. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy searches for files that are 60mb and less and contain the following extensions: .doc, .docx, .xls, .xlsx, .ppt, .pptx, .exe, .zip, and .rar. Zebrocy also runs the echo %APPDATA% command to list the contents of the directory. Zebrocy can obtain the current execution path as well as perform drive enumeration. Software 'AvosLocker': AvosLocker has searched for files and directories on a compromised network. Software 'PLEAD': PLEAD has the ability to list drives and files on the compromised host. Software 'Okrum': Okrum has used DriveLetterView to enumerate drive information. Software 'ObliqueRAT': ObliqueRAT has the ability to recursively enumerate files on an infected endpoint. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT can list file and directory information. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can parse the hard drive on a compromised host to identify specific file extensions. Software 'Troll Stealer': Troll Stealer can enumerate and collect items from local drives and folders. Software 'QuietSieve': QuietSieve can search files on the target host by extension, including doc, docx, xls, rtf, odt, txt, jpg, pdf, rar, zip, and 7z. Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT has the ability to gather a list of files and directories on the infected system. Software 'ODAgent': ODAgent can identify the current working directory. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to retrieve a list of files in a given directory as well as drives and drive types. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin will check to see if the initial executing script is located on the user's Desktop as an anti-analysis check. Software 'Peppy': Peppy can identify specific files for exfiltration. Software 'Psylo': Psylo has commands to enumerate all storage devices and to find all files that start with a particular string. Software 'Doki': Doki has resolved the path of a process PID to use as a script argument. Software 'ZIPLINE': ZIPLINE can find and append specific files on Ivanti Connect Secure VPNs based upon received commands. Software 'BlackMould': BlackMould has the ability to find files on the targeted system. Software 'CreepyDrive': CreepyDrive can specify the local file path to upload files from. Software 'POWRUNER': POWRUNER may enumerate user directories on a victim. Software 'KeyBoy': KeyBoy has a command to launch a file browser or explorer on the system. Software 'Dtrack': Dtrack can list files on available disk volumes. Software 'BACKSPACE': BACKSPACE allows adversaries to search for files. Software 'DDKONG': DDKONG lists files on the victim’s machine. Software 'Denis': Denis has several commands to search directories for files. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail can search its staging directory for output files it has produced. Software 'INC Ransomware': INC Ransomware can receive command line arguments to encrypt specific files and directories. Software 'OSX/Shlayer': OSX/Shlayer has used the command appDir="$(dirname $(dirname "$currentDir"))" and $(dirname "$(pwd -P)") to construct installation paths. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor has the ability to search the compromised host for files. Software 'Cryptoistic': Cryptoistic can scan a directory to identify files for deletion. Software 'DarkGate': Some versions of DarkGate search for the hard-coded folder C:\Program Files\e Carte Bleue. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub has the ability to only encrypt specific files. Software 'CORALDECK': CORALDECK searches for specified files. Software 'HTTPBrowser': HTTPBrowser is capable of listing files, folders, and drives on a victim. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has searched for files within the victim environment for encryption and exfiltration. Medusa Ransomware has also identified files associated with remote management services. Software 'SameCoin': SameCoin can list all system files and can avoid wiping specific directories such as Program Files, Windows, and Users. Software 'ChChes': ChChes collects the victim's %TEMP% directory path and version of Internet Explorer. Software 'Pasam': Pasam creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can retrieve lists of files. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can recursively enumerate files in an operator-provided directory. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can use a file exfiltration tool to collect recently changed files with specific extensions. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can list information about files in a directory and recently opened or used documents. InvisiMole can also search for specific files by supplied file mask. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon lists the directories for Desktop, program files, and the user’s recently accessed files. Software 'Playcrypt': Playcrypt can avoid encrypting files with a .PLAY, .exe, .msi, .dll, .lnk, or .sys file extension. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to search for .txt, .ppt, .hwp, .pdf, and .doc files in specified directories. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb's loader can check for the FoggyWeb backdoor .pri file on a compromised AD FS server. Software 'Cannon': Cannon can obtain victim drive information as well as a list of folders in C:\Program Files. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu searches for various filesystem paths to determine what banking applications are installed on the victim’s machine. Software 'WastedLocker': WastedLocker can enumerate files and directories just prior to encryption. Software 'NDiskMonitor': NDiskMonitor can obtain a list of all files and directories as well as logical drives. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list file and directory information. Software 'Akira _v2': Akira _v2 can target specific files and folders for encryption. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea collects information about available drives, default browser, desktop file list, My Documents, Internet history, program files, and root of available drives. It also searches for ICS-related software files. Software 'Derusbi': Derusbi is capable of obtaining directory, file, and drive listings. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape searches for the Kubernetes config file and other related files using a regular expression. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can enumerate files on a compromised host. Software 'MobileOrder': MobileOrder has a command to upload to its C2 server information about files on the victim mobile device, including SD card size, installed app list, SMS content, contacts, and calling history. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can enumerate files and collect associated metadata. Software 'ccf32': ccf32 can parse collected files to identify specific file extensions. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to index files from drives, user profiles, and removable drives. Software 'Prestige': Prestige can traverse the file system to discover files to encrypt by identifying specific extensions defined in a hardcoded list. Software 'Gomir': Gomir collects information about directory and file structures, including total number of subdirectories, total number of files, and total size of files on infected systems. Software 'SOUNDBITE': SOUNDBITE is capable of enumerating and manipulating files and directories. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can check for the existence of files, including its own components, as well as retrieve a list of logical drives. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can exclude specific directories and files from encryption. Software 'StreamEx': StreamEx has the ability to enumerate drive types. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can search for specific files. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN can search, read, write, move, and execute files. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO has the ability to designate specific files and folders to encryption. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot searches the system for all of the following file extensions: .avi, .mov, .mkv, .mpeg, .mpeg4, .mp4, .mp3, .wav, .ogg, .jpeg, .jpg, .png, .bmp, .gif, .tiff, .ico, .xlsx, and .zip. It can also obtain browsing history, cookies, and plug-in information. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can search for files and directories. Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has searched for .ldb and .log files stored in browser extension directories for collection and exfiltration. Software 'LookBack': LookBack can retrieve file listings from the victim machine. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to gather metadata from a file and to search for file and directory names. Software 'SplatCloak': SplatCloak has used Windows API to identify files associated with Windows Defender and Kaspersky. Software 'Kasidet': Kasidet has the ability to search for a given filename on a victim. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can collect directory and file lists. Software 'IceApple': The IceApple Directory Lister module can list information about files and directories including creation time, last write time, name, and size. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT has the ability to collect drive and file information on an infected machine. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun can enumerate information about files from the infected system, including file size, attributes, creation time, last access time, and write time. Rising Sun can enumerate the compilation timestamp of Windows executable files. Software 'SPACESHIP': SPACESHIP identifies files and directories for collection by searching for specific file extensions or file modification time. Software 'Penquin': Penquin can use the command code do_vslist to send file names, size, and status to C2. Software 'Machete': Machete produces file listings in order to search for files to be exfiltrated. Software 'ROADSWEEP': ROADSWEEP can enumerate files on infected devices and avoid encrypting files with .exe, .dll, .sys, .lnk, or . lck extensions. Software 'RTM': RTM can check for specific files and directories associated with virtualization and malware analysis. Software 'KONNI': A version of KONNI searches for filenames created with a previous version of the malware, suggesting different versions targeted the same victims and the versions may work together. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves can enumerate and search for files and directories. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can enumerate directories on a compromise host. Software 'AcidPour': AcidPour can identify specific files and directories within the Linux operating system corresponding with storage devices for follow-on wiping activity, similar to AcidRain. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has identified specific files and directories within targeted hosts and systems for modification, execution, collection and exfiltration. Software 'AcidRain': AcidRain identifies specific files and directories in the Linux operating system associated with storage devices. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot can search a compromised host for specific files. Software 'Nebulae': Nebulae can list files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'FruitFly': FruitFly looks for specific files and file types. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET has used `mdfind` to enumerate a list of apps known to grant screen sharing permissions and leverages a module to run the command `ls -la ~/Desktop`. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can recursively search for files in folders and collects files from the desktop with certain extensions. Software 'PACEMAKER': PACEMAKER can parse `/proc/"process_name"/cmdline` to look for the string `dswsd` within the command line. Software 'Remexi': Remexi searches for files on the system. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can execute enum to enumerate files in storage on a compromised system. Software 'MultiLayer Wiper': MultiLayer Wiper generates a list of all files and paths on the fixed drives of an infected system, enumerating all files on the system except specific folders defined in a hardcoded list. Software 'Cheerscrypt': Cheerscrypt can search for log and VMware-related files with .log, .vmdk, .vmem, .vswp, and .vmsn extensions. Software 'Prikormka': A module in Prikormka collects information about the paths, size, and creation time of files with specific file extensions, but not the actual content of the file. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can target directories on victim machines for file collection. Software 'Dacls': Dacls can scan directories on a compromised host. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda searches for specific directories on the victim’s machine. Software 'Rover': Rover automatically searches for files on local drives based on a predefined list of file extensions. Software 'FatDuke': FatDuke can enumerate directories on target machines. Software 'ShimRat': ShimRat can list directories. Software 'Pisloader': Pisloader has commands to list drives on the victim machine and to list file information for a given directory. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer identifies target files and directories for collection based on a configuration file. Software 'LITTLELAMB.WOOLTEA': LITTLELAMB.WOOLTEA can monitor for system upgrade events by checking for the presence of `/tmp/data/root/dev`. Software 'MacMa': MacMa can search for a specific file on the compromised computer and can enumerate files in Desktop, Downloads, and Documents folders. Software 'Royal': Royal can identify specific files and directories to exclude from the encryption process. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has searched the Program Files directories for specific folders and has searched for strings related to its mutexes. Software 'Megazord': Megazord can ignore specified directories for encryption. Software 'SharpDisco': SharpDisco can identify recently opened files by using an LNK format parser to extract the original file path from LNK files found in either `%USERPROFILE%\Recent` (Windows XP) or `%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Recent` (newer Windows versions) . Software 'BlackCat': BlackCat can enumerate files for encryption. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader recursively searches through directories for files. Software 'UPPERCUT': UPPERCUT has the capability to gather the victim's current directory. Software 'BADNEWS': BADNEWS identifies files with certain extensions from USB devices, then copies them to a predefined directory. Software 'DustySky': DustySky scans the victim for files that contain certain keywords and document types including PDF, DOC, DOCX, XLS, and XLSX, from a list that is obtained from the C2 as a text file. It can also identify logical drives for the infected machine. Software 'MESSAGETAP': MESSAGETAP checks for the existence of two configuration files (keyword_parm.txt and parm.txt) and attempts to read the files every 30 seconds. Software 'Babuk': Babuk has the ability to enumerate files on a targeted system. Software 'China Chopper': China Chopper's server component can list directory contents. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can identify files with .doc, .docx, .ppt, .pptx, .xls, .xlsx, and .pdf extensions and specific timestamps for collection. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can search for specific files on a compromised system. Software 'TYPEFRAME': TYPEFRAME can search directories for files on the victim’s machine. Software 'StealBit': StealBit can be configured to exfiltrate specific file types. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has enumerated directories containing vulnerability testing and cyber related content and facilities data such as construction drawings.Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/. Group 'Contagious Interview': Contagious Interview has conducted key word searches within files and directories on a compromised hosts to identify files for exfiltration. Group 'UNC3886': UNC3886 has used `vmtoolsd.exe` to enumerate files on guest machines. Group 'Patchwork': A Patchwork payload has searched all fixed drives on the victim for files matching a specified list of extensions. Group 'Sowbug': Sowbug identified and extracted all Word documents on a server by using a command containing * .doc and *.docx. The actors also searched for documents based on a specific date range and attempted to identify all installed software on a victim. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group macros can scan for Microsoft Word and Excel files to inject with additional malicious macros. Gamaredon Group has also used its backdoors to automatically list interesting files (such as Office documents) found on a system. Gamaredon Group has also identified directory trees, folders and files on the compromised host. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has searched for files within the victim environment for encryption and exfiltration. Medusa Group has also identified files associated with remote management services. Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has used a batch script to gather folder and file names from victim hosts. Group 'APT38': APT38 have enumerated files and directories, or searched in specific locations within a compromised host. Group 'APT28': APT28 has used Forfiles to locate PDF, Excel, and Word documents during collection. The group also searched a compromised DCCC computer for specific terms. Group 'Inception': Inception used a file listing plugin to collect information about file and directories both on local and remote drives. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has run commands to check the content of folders on compromised hosts and has specifically targeted files with .doc, .ppt, .xls, .jtd, .eml, .xps, and .pdf extensions. During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace enumerated file system details in compromised environments. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has searched for and collected files on local and network drives. Group 'Velvet Ant': Velvet Ant has enumerated local files and folders on victim devices. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has utilized multiple commands to identify data of interest in file and directory listings. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following commands after exploiting a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about files and directories: dir c:\ >> %temp%\download dir "c:\Documents and Settings" >> %temp%\download dir "c:\Program Files\" >> %temp%\download dir d:\ >> %temp%\download Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang uses command-line interaction to search files and directories. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has enumerated files on a compromised host. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used malware to collect information on files and directories. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider Spider enumerates a target organization for files and directories of interest, including source code, user provisioning, MFA device registration, network diagrams, and shared credentials in documents or spreadsheets. Group 'Aoqin Dragon': Aoqin Dragon has run scripts to identify file formats including Microsoft Word. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has monitored files' modified time. Group 'Dark Caracal': Dark Caracal collected file listings of all default Windows directories. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware that checked if the ProgramData folder had folders or files with the keywords "Kasper," "Panda," or "ESET." Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware can list a victim's logical drives and the type, as well the total/free space of the fixed devices. Other malware can list a directory's contents. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used a script that checks `/proc/*/environ` for environment variables related to AWS. Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used commands such as `dir` to examine the local filesystem of victim machines. Group 'LuminousMoth': LuminousMoth has used malware that scans for files in the Documents, Desktop, and Download folders and in other drives. Group 'APT3': APT3 has a tool that looks for files and directories on the local file system. Group 'APT32': APT32's backdoor possesses the capability to list files and directories on a machine. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has searched the entire target system for DOC, DOCX, PPT, PPTX, XLS, XLSX, and PDF files. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used tools with the ability to search for files on a compromised host. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat has run scripts to enumerate recently modified documents having either a .pdf, .doc, .docx, .xls or .xlsx extension. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware can use a common function to identify target files by their extension, and some also enumerate files and directories, including a Destover-like variant that lists files and gathers information for all drives.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group conducted word searches within documents on a compromised host in search of security and financial matters. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has used malware that searched for files with specific patterns. Group 'Play': Play has used the Grixba information stealer to list security files and processes. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has searched compromised systems for folders of interest including those related to HR, audit and expense, and meeting memos. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has the ability to enumerate all files and directories on an infected system. Kimsuky has used a custom script with a function called CreateFileList() that can scan all filesystem drives, prioritizing C:\Users, to locate files and file extensions of interest that ultimately generates a file called `FileList.txt` saved within the victims %TEMP% Directory that contains the findings and the respective pathways. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has searched file contents on a compromised host. Group 'Confucius': Confucius has used a file stealer that checks the Document, Downloads, Desktop, and Picture folders for documents and images with specific extensions. Group 'APT41': APT41 has executed file /bin/pwd on exploited victims, perhaps to return architecture related information. Group 'Winnti Group': Winnti Group has used a program named ff.exe to search for specific documents on compromised hosts. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern delivered malicious JavaScript payloads capable of listing folders and emails in exploited email servers. Group 'Windigo': Windigo has used a script to check for the presence of files created by OpenSSH backdoors. Group 'APT18': APT18 can list files information for specific directories. Group 'APT5': APT5 has used the BLOODMINE utility to discover files with .css, .jpg, .png, .gif, .ico, .js, and .jsp extensions in Pulse Secure Connect logs. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used WizTree to obtain network files and directory listings. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer used a tool called MailSniper to search for files on the desktop and another utility called Sobolsoft to extract attachments from EML files. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has used the Windows `dir` command to enumerate files and directories in a victim's network. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover files in specific locations on the hard disk %TEMP% directory, the current user's desktop, the Program Files directory, and Recent. Turla RPC backdoors have also searched for files matching the lPH*.dll pattern. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has collected a list of files from the victim and uploaded it to its C2 server, and then created a new list of specific files to steal. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 obtained information about the configured Exchange virtual directory using `Get-WebServicesVirtualDirectory`. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors conducted a file listing discovery against multiple hosts to ensure locker encryption was successful. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, the threat actors used a malicious DLL to search for files with specific keywords. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used `dir c:\\` to search for files. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors leveraged commands to locate accessible file shares, backup paths, or SharePoint content. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary leveraged Claude Code to identify sensitive data within the victim environment for extraction. Campaign 'Salesforce Data Exfiltration': During Salesforce Data Exfiltration, threat actors queried customers' Salesforce environments to identify sensitive information for exfiltration. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 obtained information about the configured Exchange virtual directory using `Get-WebServicesVirtualDirectory`. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors gathered a recursive directory listing to find files and directories of interest. Campaign 'Operation AkaiRyū': During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace enumerated file system details in compromised environments. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group conducted word searches within documents on a compromised host in search of security and financial matters. Campaign 'Night Dragon': During Night Dragon, threat actors used zwShell to establish full remote control of the connected machine and browse the victim file system. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries obtained the contents of users’ directories using `dir /s /b C:\Users` command. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/.

T1007.name: System Service Discovery
T1007.description: Adversaries may try to gather information about registered local system services. Adversaries may obtain information about services using tools as well as OS utility commands such as sc query, tasklist /svc, systemctl --type=service, and net start. Adversaries may also gather information about schedule tasks via commands such as `schtasks` on Windows or `crontab -l` on Linux and macOS.(Citation: Elastic Security Labs GOSAR 2024)(Citation: SentinelLabs macOS Malware 2021)(Citation: Splunk Linux Gormir 2024)(Citation: Aquasec Kinsing 2020) Adversaries may use the information from [System Service Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1007) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions.
T1007.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of System Service Discovery Commands Across OS Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 1325': Enumeration of services via native CLI tools (e.g., `sc query`, `tasklist /svc`, `net start`) or API calls via PowerShell and WMI.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1326': Execution of service management commands like `systemctl list-units`, `service --status-all`, or direct reading of `/etc/init.d`.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1327': Discovery via launchctl commands, or process enumeration using `ps aux | grep com.apple.` to identify daemons and services.
T1007.procedures: Software 'Net': The net start command can be used in Net to find information about Windows services. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate service and service permission information. Software 'Tasklist': Tasklist can be used to discover services running on a system. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can search for modifiable services that could be used for privilege escalation. Software 'Ursnif': Ursnif has gathered information about running services. Software 'Sykipot': Sykipot may use net start to display running services. Software 'Comnie': Comnie runs the command: net start >> %TEMP%\info.dat on a victim. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can collect a list of services on a victim machine. Software 'Elise': Elise executes net start after initial communication is made to the remote server. Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA has the capability to enumerate services. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has leveraged an encoded list of services that it designates for termination. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of running services with the command tasklist /svc. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can list local services. Software 'RATANKBA': RATANKBA uses tasklist /svc to display running tasks. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT can query service configuration information. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can create and register a service for execution. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can enumerate services on compromised hosts. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer queries the system to identify existing services. Software 'LookBack': LookBack can enumerate services on the victim machine. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor can check if it is running as a service on a compromised host. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can identify specific services for termination or to be left running at execution. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell can obtain a list of the services from a system. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can obtain running services on the victim. Software 'GreyEnergy': GreyEnergy enumerates all Windows services. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST collected a list of service names that were hashed using a FNV-1a + XOR algorithm to check against similarly-hashed hardcoded blocklists. Software 'HyperBro': HyperBro can list all services and their configurations. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can check whether the service name `FAX` is present. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has obtained active services running on the victim’s system through the functions `OpenSCManagerW()` and `EnumServicesStatusExW()`. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can monitor services. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT has a feature to list the available services on the system. Software 'REvil': REvil can enumerate active services. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can query service status using QueryServiceStatusEx function. Software 'SynAck': SynAck enumerates all running services. Software 'Sardonic': Sardonic has the ability to execute the `net start` command. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to retrieve a list of services on the infected host. Software 'S-Type': S-Type runs the command net start on a victim. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information on programs and services on the victim that are configured to automatically run at startup. Software 'ZLib': ZLib has the ability to discover and manipulate Windows services. Software 'DarkTortilla': DarkTortilla can retrieve information about a compromised system's running services. Software 'Emissary': Emissary has the capability to execute the command net start to interact with services. Software 'Babuk': Babuk can enumerate all services running on a compromised host. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list running services. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can enumerate services on a victim machine. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate services. Software 'Epic': Epic uses the tasklist /svc command to list the services on the system. Software 'BitPaymer': BitPaymer can enumerate existing Windows services on the host that are configured to run as LocalSystem. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot collects a list of install programs and services on the system’s machine. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has leveraged `tasklist` to gather running services on victim host. Software 'LAMEHUG': LAMEHUG can gather service information on targeted systems. Software 'JPIN': JPIN can list running services. Software 'Dyre': Dyre has the ability to identify running services on a compromised host. Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell can check the services on the system. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has used TROJ_GETVERSION to discover system services. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has searched for services such as Alibaba Cloud Security's aliyun service and BMC Helix Cloud Security's bmc-agent service in order to disable them. Group 'Indrik Spider': Indrik Spider has used the win32_service WMI class to retrieve a list of services from the system. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has used sc query on a victim to gather information about services. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover running services and associated processes using the tasklist /svc command. Group 'Aquatic Panda': Aquatic Panda has attempted to discover services for third party EDR products. Group 'Poseidon Group': After compromising a victim, Poseidon Group discovers all running services. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has used an instrumentor script to gather the names of all services running on a victim's system. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used net start and net use for system service discovery. Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang performs service discovery using net start commands. Group 'MirrorFace': MirrorFace has used Tasklist for discovery post compromise. Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Tasklist to obtain information from a compromised host. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used `net start` to list running services. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following command following exploitation of a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about services: net start >> %temp%\download Group 'APT1': APT1 used the commands net start and tasklist to get a listing of the services on the system. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors used the `tasklist` command to search for one of its backdoors. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used the `net start` command as part of their initial reconnaissance.

mitre_evaluations.explanation: The alert explicitly shows a user 'webadmin' executing a command via 'sudo' to gain root privileges. This is the direct definition of Sudo usage.
While the alert involves root privileges, there is no evidence of systemd service manipulation or persistence creation.
The command executed was '/usr/bin/lspci', which is a tool used to list PCI devices. This is a direct example of peripheral device discovery.
This technique is specific to macOS Launch Daemons and is not relevant to the Linux-based sudo event.
This technique is specific to a macOS API and is not relevant to the Linux sudo command execution.
While the user is escalating privileges, the alert does not show active discovery of permission groups.
The execution of 'lspci' is a form of system information discovery, though it is more specifically peripheral discovery.
There is no evidence of task scheduling in the alert.
No file or directory enumeration was performed in the provided log.
No system service discovery was performed in the provided log.
